narrative:
The weekend begins with a mix of clouds and sunshine as a weak weather disturbance slowly lifts northeast through the Mid-Mississippi Valley. While most of the morning hours should remain dry, increasing daytime heating and instability will allow isolated to scattered showers and thunderstorms to develop during the afternoon and evening.

The atmosphere will become moderately unstable this afternoon with enough energy for a few stronger storms, especially west of the Mississippi River. However, wind shear remains weak, limiting storm organization. While a brief gusty wind or small hail report cannot be ruled out, widespread severe weather is not expected. Many communities will remain dry, but any storm that develops could produce brief downpours and lightning.

Shower and thunderstorm chances will diminish somewhat after sunset tonight, although a few isolated storms may continue overnight, particularly across central and eastern Missouri as the upper-level disturbance moves closer to the region.

Sunday looks to be the wettest day of the forecast period. As the disturbance passes overhead, scattered showers and thunderstorms are expected to become much more numerous during the afternoon and evening. Rain chances increase to 80 to 90 percent across much of the area. While severe weather is not anticipated, the atmosphere will contain abundant moisture, allowing storms to produce locally heavy rainfall. Areas that experience repeated rounds of thunderstorms could see ponding of water on roadways and localized flooding concerns.

The unsettled weather pattern continues into Monday. High humidity and temperatures climbing into the 80s will create another unstable atmosphere capable of producing scattered showers and thunderstorms. Once again, locally heavy rainfall will be the primary concern due to tropical-like moisture levels moving north from the Gulf of Mexico.

Looking ahead to next week, attention shifts from rain to heat. An expanding ridge of high pressure across the southern United States is expected to build toward the Midwest. This will allow temperatures to climb into the upper 80s and lower 90s beginning Tuesday, with some locations potentially reaching the middle 90s later in the week.

Combined with increasing humidity, heat index values could approach or exceed 100 degrees across portions of Missouri and Illinois from Wednesday through Friday. While there remains some uncertainty regarding exactly how strong the heat becomes, confidence is increasing that the region will experience its first significant stretch of summer-like conditions of the season.

Have outdoor plans this weekend? Keep an eye on the sky, especially Sunday and Monday, when showers and thunderstorms will be most widespread. As we move deeper into next week, prepare for a return to heat and humidity that will make it feel much more like July than early June.
